findings.description: The `index` dict built from the header (line 36) is never used; rows are indexed by hard-coded positions row[0]..row[3], so any CSV whose column order differs from id,name,amount,email silently maps wrong values to wrong fields — the column-presence check on line 37 is completely ineffective as a guard.

No bounds check before accessing row[0]..row[3]; a short row (e.g., trailing comma produces fewer fields, or a blank line yields an empty list) raises IndexError and aborts the entire parse with no error recovery or skip logic.

int(row[0]) and float(row[2]) are unguarded; a single malformed value (empty string, non-numeric text) raises ValueError that propagates out of parse(), discarding all already-processed records and giving the caller no indication of which row failed.
count_rows returns len(lines) - 1, which is -1 for a zero-line file, a nonsensical negative count that callers cannot distinguish from a valid result without their own guard.

open(path) is called without a context manager (no 'with' statement), leaking the file descriptor if an exception occurs; count_rows also loads the entire file into memory via readlines(), which will OOM on large files.

The `index` dict is dead code: it is constructed but never referenced anywhere in the method; the column-order assumption on lines 44-47 makes the REQUIRED_COLUMNS presence check (line 37) a false safety net — columns can be present in any order and the wrong data will be silently assigned.

os.path.basename strips Unix path separators but not Windows backslashes when running on Linux, so a filename like 'foo\\..\\bar' would not be stripped correctly, enabling a partial path traversal outside export_dir; deferred to critic-security for full analysis.
stream_valid accepts any string containing '@' as a valid email, including '@', '@@', or 'a@'; junk emails pass the filter silently and propagate to callers and the exported CSV without warning.

os.makedirs is called unconditionally in __init__, making instantiation fail with PermissionError for any caller that only intends to use parse() or stream_valid() and has no need for the export directory.
